items.title: Browser AI vs Cloud APIs for Image Processing
items.description:
Most online image tools follow the same pattern:

  1. upload an image;
  2. wait for a server to process it;
  3. download the result.

That model works well. Cloud APIs are convenient, scalable from a developer experience point of view, and often give very consistent

What is React?

items.title: The AI Stack For 2026: LLMs, Vector Databases, Tool Calling, Agents, And Observability
items.description:
The AI stack for 2026 is not one model, one API, or one shiny agent demo.

It is a production system: LLMs for reasoning, vector databases for memory, tool calling for action, agents for workflow, and observability for trust.

items.title: AGEF explained: a portable evidence format for AI agent sessions
items.description:
If you ship AI-assisted code in a regulated codebase and somebody asks "show me what the agent did", you have about a week before that question turns into a finding. The data exists somewhere. It is not in a single shape. It is rarely portable. It is almost never tamper evident.

items.title: The trust pipeline: three commands to run before merging an AI-assisted change
items.description:
The most common failure mode I see when teams adopt AI coding agents is not a bad diff. It is a good diff that no one can defend. The agent ran. The session closed. Three days later, somebody asks how the change came to be, and there is nothing to point at.

items.title: Finally, Private AI Characters That Can Run Offline
items.description:
Most AI character apps ask you to make a tradeoff.

You can have a fun, expressive roleplay experience, but the conversation usually runs through someone else's servers. Or you can use a local AI model for privacy, but the experience often feels like a raw chat box built for technical users
